coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of porous nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs)@g-C3N4 for highly efficient photocatalytic and anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xue-Chun
Yang, Yun-Ling
Zhang, Shao-Lin
Liu, Yu-Feng
Fu, Si-Jie
Zhu, Meng
Hu, Jian-Feng
Zhang, Zhi-Jun
Zhao, Jing-Tai
coredata.dc:description: A novel porous nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs)@g-C3N4 composite was successfully synthesized by a facile hydrothermal approach. The surface area of NCDs@g-C3N4 can reach up to 60.9 m3/g, and pores with diameter between 20–50 nm are the most widely distributed. The photocatalytic activity of NCDs@g-C3N4 toward the degradation of Methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation was remarkably higher than that of g-C3N4 and NCDs. The improved photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the fact that the addition of NCDs not only broadens the photo-response range, but also suppresses the photo-generated electrons and holes recombination of g-C3N4. Degradation rate of 2.0 wt% NCDs@g-C3N4 is about twice than that of pristine g-C3N4 at 60 min under the same conditions. In addition, at 120 min, MB was almost degraded completely by 2.0 wt% NCDs@g-C3N4 with the degradation rate as high as 97.9%, while the pristine g-C3N4 was only 69%. NCDs@g-C3N4 can be used as a potential catalyst for organic contaminants degradation considering its high photo-response range and excellent photocatalytic activity. Moreover, NCDs, g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4 also can be used as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4
Hydrothermal
NCDs
Photocatalytic
Fluorescent inks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL and UV–vis spectroscopy of NCDs
Morphology analysis of g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4
PL and UV–vis spectroscopy of g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Funding Programs for Youth Teachers of Shanghai Colleges and Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b)HRTEM of NCDs, (c) Particle size distribution diagram of the NCDs, (d) AFM images of the NCDs, the inset shows the height of the NCDs on track 1.
(a) FTIR spectra of the NCDs and its precursors, (b) Raman spectra of the NCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black), PL excitation (blue) and emission (red) spectra of the NCDs, (b) PL spectra of NCDs, the inset shows the relationship between fluorescence intensity and excitation wavelength of NCDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) TEM, HRTEM (inset) and (b) SEM images of g-C3N4, (c) TEM and (d) SEM images of NCDs@g-C3N4.
(a) PL spectra under 300 nm excitation and (b) UV–vis absorbance spectra of g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4.
(a) Degradation curve and (b) curve of kinetic reaction equation of NCDs, g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4.
Schematic illustration of the possible photocatalytic processes in the NCDs@g-C3N4 composite under visible light irradiation.

Photographs of fluorescent inks. The images of the fluorescent inks (in cuvette) and written pictures under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) g-C3N4 (a), NCDs (b) and NCDs@g-C3N4 (C), (d) Combined written pictures of three types of fluorescent inks under visible light (above) and 365 nm ultraviolet light (below).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of porous nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs)@g-C3N4 for highly efficient photocatalytic and anti-counterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200072, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Guangzhou University, Guangzhou 510006, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 200235, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, Shanghai 200235, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel porous nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs)@g-C3N4 composite was successfully synthesized by a facile hydrothermal approach. The surface area of NCDs@g-C3N4 can reach up to 60.9 m3/g, and pores with diameter between 20–50 nm are the most widely distributed. The photocatalytic activity of NCDs@g-C3N4 toward the degradation of Methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation was remarkably higher than that of g-C3N4 and NCDs. The improved photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the fact that the addition of NCDs not only broadens the photo-response range, but also suppresses the photo-generated electrons and holes recombination of g-C3N4. Degradation rate of 2.0 wt% NCDs@g-C3N4 is about twice than that of pristine g-C3N4 at 60 min under the same conditions. In addition, at 120 min, MB was almost degraded completely by 2.0 wt% NCDs@g-C3N4 with the degradation rate as high as 97.9%, while the pristine g-C3N4 was only 69%. NCDs@g-C3N4 can be used as a potential catalyst for organic contaminants degradation considering its high photo-response range and excellent photocatalytic activity. Moreover, NCDs, g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4 also can be used as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:para: It is one of the effective ways to deal with water pollution by using photocatalyst to degrade pollutants [1,2]. Among many photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted immensely global attention since it was first adopted as a metal-free polymeric semiconductor for photocatalytic water-splitting to produce H2 and O2 in 2009 [3]. It is widely used for pollutant degradation, CO2 reduction, and hydrogen production because of the intriguing features such as good chemical and thermal stability, low cost, ease of preparation and visible light response [4]. But the high recombination rates of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and low visible-light absorption efficiencies limit the photo-catalytic efficacy of pristine g-C3N4, which greatly hinders its practical applications [5]. As a rookie of the carbon family, CDs not only inherit the excellent family nature [6,7], but also show the unique properties in the field of photocatalysis. For example, due to light-induced electron transfer, wide absorption range and flexible surface state, CDs have been successfully integrated with semiconductors to improve their photocatalytic activity by decreasing electron-hole recombination and broadening the photo-absorption region [25]. Wang et al. reported that N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) exhibit 13.6 fold higher reaction rate than that of pristine g-C3N4 toward the degradation of indomethacin (IDM) under visible light irradiation [8]. Carbon dots (CDs) decorated graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) photocatalyst was synthesized via a facile impregnation-thermal method by Zhang et al. [9] for phenol degradation. They found that impregnation of CDs into g-C3N4 not only enhanced the production of photo-generated electron-hole pairs by extending the visible light absorption region due to the up-converted photoluminescence of CDs, but also facilitated electron-hole separation by band alignment in the g-C3N4/CDs junction, thus yielded more holes, •O2- and •OH radicals to promote phenol degradation. In addition, there are many reports about the CDs modified g-C3N4 for H2 evolution [10,11], degradation of methyl orange [2] and so on [12,12b]. However, these current reports are only about how CDs affect the performance (optical properties, catalytic properties, etc.) of g-C3N4. It is well known that there are many important factors affecting photocatalytic performance, which are not only the photo-response range and electron-hole separation rate but also morphology. Larger specific surface area and richer void structure will greatly improve photocatalytic efficiency. So it is important to study and develop the effect of CDs on the morphology of g-C3N4.

Here we synthesized a novel porous nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs)@g-C3N4 composite successfully by a facile hydrothermal approach, for the degradation of MB under visible light irradiation. It was found the first time that NCDs have an incredible effect on pore making of g-C3N4. The structural and optical properties of NCDs and composite were analyzed. The effects of NCDs content on the properties of the NCDs@g-C3N4 composite were investigated. And the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity was speculated. In addition, we have developed NCDs, g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4 as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting applications. This work played a pioneering role in studying the effect of NCDs on the morphology of g-C3N4.

P-phenylenediamine and urea were purchased from Aladdin-reagent Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the materials are used as received without further treatment. Material purification was carried out using 10 kDa cutoff membranes (Amicon Ultra-4, Millipore).

In a typical procedure [13], 3.0 g of p-phenylenediamine was combined with a certain quantity of urea and dissolved in 50 mL of DI water. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained red-brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles and the supernatant was kept for dialysis for 12 h. After removing solvents and further freeze drying, red-brown powder was obtained.

The bulk g-C3N4 was prepared using melamine as the precursor in the muffle furnace with the chimney connecting to the atmosphere. Initially, melamine (2 g) was calcined at 550 °C for 4 h where the furnace was preheated at a constant rate of 2 °C min−1. The brilliant yellow bulk g-C3N4 was obtained. Subsequently, 500 mg bulk g-C3N4 was ground into powder and heated at 550 °C for 1 h. The prepared sample was light yellow. 1 g g-C3N4 was placed in 100 mL of deionized water to ultrasound at 500 W for 4 h.

The 5 mL DI water contained a certain quantity of NCDs and 50 mL g-C3N4 was mixed and stirred for 30 min. The mixture solution was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h to obtain the NCDs@g-C3N4 photocatalyst. Different NCDs@g-C3N4 samples with NCDs at mass percentages of 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 and 2.5% were prepared through the same procedure.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the NCDs are monodispersed with uniform sizes, which is beneficial for the emission of fluorescence with a single fixed wavelength (about 605 nm) which exhibits excitation wavelength independence (Fig. 3(b)). Their average lateral size determined by TEM is about 5 nm with a narrow size distribution (Fig. 1(c)). What's more, the high-resolution HRTEM image (Fig. 1(b)) shows that NCDs exhibited well-resolved lattice fringes with a (100) spacing of 0.21 nm. This is consistent with previous reports [16]. In addition, the AFM image shows that the average height of NCDs is about 3 nm (Fig. 1(d)).

The FT-IR spectra reveal that the NCDs mainly contain methylene (2890, 2970 and 1380 cm−1), −CONH− (1690 cm−1), CC (1499 cm−1), aromatic C−N (1271 cm−1) and C-OH (1090 cm−1) functional groups or chemical bonds (Fig. 2(a)) [8,13]. These FT-IR assignments are further verified by XPS analysis. For instance, the high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum exhibits characteristic peaks of CC, C-O/C−N, CN, and −CONH− at 283.6, 284.7, 287.8, and 288.4 eV, respectively (Fig. S1(a)) [17]. The high-resolution N1 s XPS spectrum exhibits the distinctive peaks of C-N-C and N-(C)3 at 398.6 and 400.4 eV, respectively (Fig. S1(b)). From the Raman spectra (Fig. 2(b)), two characteristic D and G bands were observed at 1370 cm−1 and 1525 cm−1. And the intensities of the ordered G band and the disordered D band are basically equal, indicating that NCDs have a certain degree of crystallization and rich defect [18].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL and UV–vis spectroscopy of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the superior optical properties of NCDs, UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were measured and shown in Fig. 3 . From the UV–vis absorption (Fig. 3(a)), it can be seen that NCDs have strong absorption in the longer wavelength range of 300–550 nm, which exhibits long-wavelength emission characteristics of the NCDs [19]. Notably, the self-absorption of NCDs is quite small because of small overlap between absorption and emission spectra, which is beneficial for efficient fluorescence emission [18]. The emission peak of NCDs is located at about 605 nm. As the increases of excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission intensity of NCDs shows an enhanced trend. The NCDs can be excited with a wide range of wavelengths and the maximum emission peak position is virtually independent of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 3(b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology analysis of g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the morphologys of g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4 were observed in detail. The TEM images in Fig. 4(a) reveal the 2D lamellar structure of g-C3N4 and the SEM images in Fig. 4(b) is macro particle size distribution of g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the synthesized g-C3N4 shows typical two-dimensional layered structure with a lattice spacing of about 0.245 nm [20]. Fortunately, compared to the traditional template hole making method, the complex NCDs@g-C3N4 prepared by one step hydrothermal method still has rich pore structure [21]. It was found the first time that NCDs have an incredible effect on pore making of g-C3N4. The NCDs are evenly distributed on the g-C3N4 nanosheet (Fig. S3). The surface area of pristine g-C3N4 is 40.9 m3/g. In contrast, the surface area of NCDs@g-C3N4 can reach up to 60.9 m3/g by BET method (Fig. S2), and pores with diameters between 20 and 50 nm are the most widely distributed. The possible formation process of pore structures of NCDs@g-C3N4 is proposed. When the NCDs is added to the g-C3N4 aqueous solution,the solution shows weakly alkaline. At this time, the OH– in the aqueous solution undergoes the redox reaction with C of g-C3N4 [26]. What's more, the modification of NCDs leads to the decreased content of CC double bond in NCDs@g-C3N4 (Fig. S1 (d)), compared to g-C3N4 from Fig. S1 (c). Based on this result, it is speculated that C element which undergoes the redox reaction in g-C3N4 exists in the form of CC double bond. The decreased amount of CC double bond results in the collapse of local structure constructed by the CC double bond of g-C3N4, and then the loss of the C element in g-C3N4. As a consequence, the rich pore structure of NCDs@g-C3N4 is developed. And from the XPS spectra (Fig. S1(c)–(d)), subsequent to NCDs doping, the N-C=N (287.8 eV) [20,22] relative content of NCDs@g-C3N4 increased slightly compared to the g-C3N4. It implies that NCDs and g-C3N4 may be bound together by the N-C=N bond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL and UV–vis spectroscopy of g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently, PL analysis was conducted to investigate the separation efficiency of photo-generated electrons and holes. Lower fluorescence emission intensity indicates a reduced electron–hole recombination rate and higher photocatalytic activity. The PL emission spectra of pristine g-C3N4 in Fig. 5(a) displays a strong emission peak at about 450 and 365 nm when excited at a wavelength of 300 nm. Compared to g-C3N4, the PL intensity of the NCDs@g-C3N4 remarkably decreased in alignment with the additional NCDs content, indicating that the NCDs might promote the transfer of charges and inhibit the electron–hole recombination rate of g-C3N4. Further, the emission peak of the NCDs@g-C3N4 showed slight blue-shift at 366 nm because of the strong emission peak at 350 nm of NCDs compared to the pristine g-C3N4. Meanwhile since the NCDs appeared obvious fluorescence in the longer wavelength range of above 500 nm, making the emission spectrum of NCDs@g-C3N4 after 500 nm more placid. Moreover, the absorption spectra (Fig. 5(b)) also show that the absorption intensity of the composite increases obviously, which means the utilization rate of natural light is higher, compared with pure g-C3N4. Thus, the NCDs have no adverse effect on the catalytic performance of g-C3N4 itself, but it can effectively inhibit the electron hole recombination rate, and broaden the utilization range and intensity of g-C3N4. Moreover, a large number of pore structures appeared in the composites synthesized by hydrothermal method, which greatly increases the specific surface area of g-C3N4. All the reasons aforementioned will lead to the improved photocatalytic activity of NCDs@g-C3N4.

The photocatalytic activity of all NCDs@g-C3N4 was remarkably higher than that of g-C3N4 and NCDs toward the degradation of Methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation (Fig. 6(a)). The degradation rate of the 2.0 wt% NCDs@g-C3N4 was 82% at 60 min, which is much higher than that of NCDs (43%) and pristine g-C3N4 (29%). The degradation rate of 2.0 wt% NCDs@g-C3N4 was increased by 182% compared to g-C3N4 and was 91% higher than that of NCDs, respectively. In 120 min, MB was almost degraded completely using 2.0 wt% NCDs@g-C3N4 with the degradation rate as high as 97.9%, while the pristine g-C3N4 and NCDs degraded only 47 and 72%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6(b), before 120 min, the samples followed the first-order kinetics ln (A 0/A
t ) = kt, where A
t and A 0 were the UV–vis absorption intensity of uncatalyzed MB at each sampling and initial catalysis, respectively. The kinetics data were also calculated and listed in Table S1, as R 2 was goodness of fit and the slope k was the reaction rate constant. The closer value of R 2 is to 1, the more accurate the fitting equation is. And reaction rate of 2.0 wt% NCDs@g-C3N4 is the fastest 0.0314, which exhibits 5.7 fold higher than that of pristine g-C3N4 and 2.9 fold higher than that of NCDs. It is due to the addition of NCDs not only broadens the photo-response range of g-C3N4 but also suppresses the photogenerated electrons and holes recombination of g-C3N4. Moreover, richer holes structure was given to NCDs@g-C3N4, which means more MB was adsorbed meanwhile more photocatalytic reaction sites were provided. Then more active groups promoted the rapid degradation of MB such as O ions can be generated (Fig. 7 ). With the increase of the NCDs adding amount, the degradation rate of the NCDs@g-C3N4 has not been obviously improved. It can be seen that the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4 can hardly be enhanced with the adding of NCDs. That means a very small NCDs adding can greatly improve the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4. So NCDs play a role of strong energy conversion components used in the design of photocatalyst. The excellent catalytic performance of NCDs@g-C3N4 can provide tremendous potential for organic degradation [6,23].

In addition, we have developed anti-counterfeiting applications of NCDs, g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 8(a)–(c), the uniform mixture of g-C3N4, NCDs and NCDs@g-C3N4 with low content (1.2 wt%) is white, red and gray under visible light while show bright blue, orange and cyan fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp respectively. It can be directly used as ink to write fluorescent letter (Fig. 8(d)). In natural light, the writings are unclear yellow-brown. However, these fluorescences are clear, strong and brightly under 365 nm UV light. Therefore, these three samples have potential as fluorescent inks [24].

Abundant pore structure of NCDs@g-C3N4 with larger surface area was obtained by a simple hydrothermal method due to the addition of NCDs. The photodegradation rate of g-C3N4 can be increased by 182% at 60 min when using 2.0% of NCDs. NCDs can be widely used as a light energy conversion components to supplement and improve the catalytic activity of a number of photocatalyst and even change the morphology of the catalyst for increasing its surface area, in order to create a green and healthy green life with purer domestic water. Moreover, NCDs, g-C3N4 and NCDs@g-C3N4 also can be used as fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and so on.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51772185, 11104298, 51402184, U1832159, 111 Plan (D16002), Shanghai University Innovation Program and Shanghai School Young Teacher Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.05.367.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Decoration of TiO2/g-C3N4 Z-scheme by carbon dots as a novel photocatalyst with improved visible-light photocatalytic performance for the degradation of enrofloxacin
